description: This figure presents a comparative scatter plot analyzing the apparent densities of alloying elements in ferritic steels derived from regression modeling against pure elemental densities and literature values. The y-axis displays density in kg/m³ ranging from 0 to 16,000, while the x-axis lists individual elements: Fe, Si, Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni, V, S, and Cu. Three data series are differentiated by symbols: filled squares with error bars represent "Apparent Density, Regression" (the inverse of regression coefficients r_i with 95% probability error bars), open squares denote pure elemental "Density," and triangles indicate "Average of Apparent Density" from literature. The visualization reveals that Cu exhibits the highest apparent density (~11,800 kg/m³) followed by Mo (~10,500 kg/m³), indicating these elements increase ferrite density, whereas S shows the lowest values (~2,500 kg/m³), signifying a strong density-reducing effect. The caption states the regression model achieves R² = 0.994 with maximum deviation of 4.2 kg/m³ and average deviation of 1.4 kg/m³. The chart demonstrates close agreement between regression results and literature values for Cr, Cu, Fe, Mn, Mo, Ni, S, and Si, while V shows deviation, supporting the validity of the thermodynamic modeling approach for steel density prediction.
